**Step 2:** Compute Founder A's stake after the angel investment.

First, determine the new total number of shares after issuance of 2,500 fresh shares:

$$
N_{\text{new total}} = N_{\text{old}} + N_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{new total}} = 10{,}000 + 2{,}500 = 12{,}500 \text{ shares}
$$

Founder A still holds 10,000 shares (her absolute holding is unchanged), but the *base* has grown. So:

$$
\text{Stake}_{\text{new}} = \frac{10{,}000}{12{,}500} \times 100
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\frac{10{,}000}{12{,}500} = 0.8
$$

Converting to percentage:

$$
\text{Stake}_{\text{new}} = 0.8 \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the dilution.

$$
\text{Dilution} = \text{Stake}_{\text{initial}} - \text{Stake}_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
\text{Dilution} = 100\% - 80\% = 20\%
$$

> **Final Answer:** Founder A's stake fell from **100% → 80%**, a dilution of **20 percentage points**. The angel investor now owns 2,500 / 12,500 = **20%**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — EPS, P/E Ratio & Market Capitalization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:**
> "BlueOrbit Technologies" has the following data for FY 2024–25:
> - Net Profit = **₹50,00,000**
> - Preference Dividend = **₹5,00,000**
> - Number of Equity Shares Outstanding = **5,00,000 shares**
> - Market Price per Share = **₹120**
> Compute: (a) EPS, (b) P/E Ratio, (c) Dividend Yield if dividend per share is **₹6**.

### Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Part (a) — Earnings Per Share (EPS):**

The formula accounts for the fact that preference shareholders must be paid their fixed dividend *before* equity shareholders receive anything.

$$
\text{EPS} = \frac{\text{Net Profit} - \text{Preference Dividend}}{N_{\text{equity shares}}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{EPS} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000}
$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$
50{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 45{,}00{,}000
$$

Dividing:

$$
\text{EPS} = \frac{45{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 9
$$

**EPS = ₹9 per share.**

**Part (b) — P/E Ratio:**

$$
\text{P/E} = \frac{\text{Market Price per Share}}{\text{EPS}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{P/E} = \frac{120}{9} = 13.33
$$

**P/E Ratio ≈ 13.33.** Interpretation: investors are willing to pay ₹13.33 for every ₹1 of BlueOrbit's earnings.

**Part (c) — Dividend Yield:**

$$
\text{Yield} = \frac{\text{Dividend per Share}}{\text{Market Price per Share}} \times 100
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{Yield} = \frac{6}{120} \times 100
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\frac{6}{120} = 0.05
$$

Converting to percentage:

$$
\text{Yield} = 0.05 \times 100 = 5\%
$$

**Dividend Yield = 5% p.a.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stake Decision Framework

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix when deciding *how much* stake to dilute during a fundraising round.

| **Funding Round** | **Typical Stake Diluted** | **Investor Type** | **Founder's Recommended Post-Money Stake** |
|-------------------|---------------------------|--------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Bootstrapping / Friends & Family | 0% – 5% | Personal Network | **90% – 100%** |
| Pre-Seed / Idea Stage | 5% – 15% | Angel Investors | **70% – 90%** |
| Seed Round | 10% – 25% | Seed VCs | **50% – 70%** |
| Series A | 15% – 25% | Institutional VCs | **35% – 55%** |
| Series B / C | 10% – 20% | Growth Equity / PE | **20% – 40%** |
| IPO | 25% – 40% | Public Shareholders | **15% – 30%** (founder-promoter) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *face value* with *market value*. Face value (₹10) is fixed on the certificate; market value (e.g., ₹120) is what the share trades at on the stock exchange. Calculations of **Market Capitalization** use market price, **not** face value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Share Capital Structure — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the hierarchical structure of share capital inside a company, showing how Authorized, Issued, and Paid-up capital inter-relate.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Authorized Share Capital Maximum capital company CAN issue] --> B[Issued Share Capital Portion offered to public and promoters]
    B --> C[Subscribed Share Capital Portion actually applied for by investors]
    C --> D[Paid-up Share Capital Portion for which money is RECEIVED]
    D --> E1[Equity Share Capital Voting rights + Variable dividend]
    D --> E2[Preference Share Capital Fixed dividend + Priority on liquidation]
    E2 --> E3[Cumulative Preference Arrears accumulate if unpaid]
    E2 --> E4[Convertible Preference Option to swap for equity]
    E2 --> E5[Redeemable Preference Company buys back after term]
```

---

## 4.2 Stake Flow — From Founder to Public Shareholder

This diagram illustrates how ownership (stake) flows and dilutes as the company progresses through funding rounds.

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[Founder 100 percent stake] -->|Issues 20 percent to Angel| F2[Founder 80 percent + Angel 20 percent]
    F2 -->|Series A issues 25 percent fresh shares| F3[Founder 60 percent + Angel 20 percent + VC 20 percent]
    F3 -->|Series B issues 15 percent fresh shares| F4[Founder 51 percent + Angel 17 percent + VC 17 percent + Growth 15 percent]
    F4 -->|IPO issues 30 percent to public| F5[Founder 36 percent + Angel 12 percent + VC 12 percent + Growth 10 percent + Public 30 percent]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** At each stage, the **total pie grows**, but the **founder's slice shrinks** in percentage terms — yet the **absolute value** typically grows because the company is now worth more.

---

## 4.3 Stake Classification Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Stake in a Company] --> S1[Majority Stake Greater than 50 percent]
    S --> S2[Minority Stake Less than 50 percent]
    S1 --> S1a[Controlling Stake Above 50 percent = Decision-making power]
    S1 --> S1b[Super Majority 75 percent plus = Special resolutions]
    S2 --> S2a[Blocking Minority 25 percent plus 1 share = Veto power]
    S2 --> S2b[Significant Minority 10 to 25 percent = Board influence]
    S2 --> S2c[Nominal Stake Less than 10 percent = Passive investor]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a share. Distinguish between equity shares and preference shares in any three parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **share** is an indivisible unit of the capital of a company, representing a fractional ownership interest held by a shareholder.

| **Parameter** | **Equity Share** | **Preference Share** |
|---------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Dividend Priority | Paid *last* (residual) | Paid *first* at a fixed rate |
| Voting Rights | Yes — one vote per share | Usually *no* voting rights |
| Capital Repayment on Winding Up | Last claim (residual) | Repaid *before* equity holders |
| Risk-Return Profile | High risk, high return | Lower risk, fixed return |

*(Allocate 1 mark each for any three distinct points; 0.5 mark for the basic definition.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by a 'controlling stake'? Why is it important for an entrepreneur-founder to retain it?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **controlling stake** is an ownership interest of **more than 50%** of the voting shares of a company, which gives the holder the power to pass ordinary resolutions and direct the strategic decisions of the firm.

**Importance to an entrepreneur-founder:**
1. **Decision-making authority** — the founder can implement their vision without being overruled by minority investors.
2. **Protection from hostile takeovers** — a controlling stake cannot be acquired by a competitor without the founder's consent.
3. **Attractive to lenders and partners** — lenders prefer companies with a stable, identifiable decision-maker.

*(1 mark for definition; 2 marks for any two well-explained reasons.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question carries an **internal choice**. Two independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### 🔷 Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define stake. Explain any **four types of stakes** an entrepreneur may encounter, with one-line examples each. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A company has **8,00,000 equity shares** of face value ₹10, trading at a market price of **₹85**. The net profit for the year is **₹72,00,000** and the preference dividend paid is **₹12,00,000**. The company declares a total equity dividend of **₹24,00,000**.
Compute:
   (i) Earnings Per Share (EPS)
   (ii) Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio
   (iii) Dividend per Share (DPS)
   (iv) Dividend Yield
   (v) Market Capitalization *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Stake Types (7 Marks)

**Definition:** A *stake* is the percentage of ownership or financial interest an individual or entity holds in a business enterprise.

**Four Types of Stakes:**

1. **Founder's Stake** – Ownership held by the original entrepreneur(s) who conceptualized and registered the company. Example: The two co-founders of Flipkart held the founder's stake before the Walmart acquisition.

2. **Angel/VC Stake** – Acquired by external investors in exchange for capital infusion during seed or growth rounds. Example: Accel Partners' stake in Facebook during its early stages.

3. **Employee Stake (ESOPs)** – Granted to employees as part of compensation, aligning their interests with company growth. Example: Infosys ESOPs granted to senior developers.

4. **Strategic/Corporate Stake** – Acquired by another company for strategic reasons (synergy, supply-chain integration). Example: Microsoft's stake in OpenAI.

*(1 mark for definition; 1.5 marks each for any four types with example.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{equity}} = 8{,}00{,}000$ shares
- Face value = ₹10, Market price = ₹85
- Net Profit = ₹72,00,000
- Preference Dividend = ₹12,00,000
- Total Equity Dividend = ₹24,00,000

**(i) EPS:**

$$
\text{EPS} = \frac{\text{Net Profit} - \text{Preference Dividend}}{N_{\text{equity shares}}}
$$

$$
\text{EPS} = \frac{72{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 7.5
$$

**EPS = ₹7.5 per share.** *(1.5 marks)*

**(ii) P/E Ratio:**

$$
\text{P/E} = \frac{\text{Market Price}}{\text{EPS}} = \frac{85}{7.5} = 11.33
$$

**P/E Ratio ≈ 11.33.** *(1.5 marks)*

**(iii) Dividend Per Share:**

$$
\text{DPS} = \frac{\text{Total Equity Dividend}}{N_{\text{equity shares}}} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 3
$$

**DPS = ₹3 per share.** *(1.5 marks)*

**(iv) Dividend Yield:**

$$
\text{Yield} = \frac{\text{DPS}}{\text{Market Price}} \times 100 = \frac{3}{85} \times 100 = 3.53\%
$$

**Dividend Yield ≈ 3.53% p.a.** *(1.5 marks)*

**(v) Market Capitalization:**

$$
\text{Mcap} = N_{\text{total}} \times \text{Market Price} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 85 = 68{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

**Market Cap = ₹68 crore.** *(1 mark)*

---

### 🔷 Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **equity dilution**. A founder owns **12,000 shares** of a company that has **15,000 shares outstanding**. The company issues **5,000 new shares** to a venture capital firm. Calculate:
   (i) The founder's stake *before* and *after* the issue.
   (ii) The percentage dilution suffered by the founder.
   (iii) The amount raised by the company if the issue price is **₹50 per share**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **rights and liabilities of a shareholder** under the Companies Act, 2013. Highlight any **six** key rights. *(7 Marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Dilution Analysis (7 Marks)

**Concept of Equity Dilution (1 mark):**
Equity dilution occurs when a company issues *additional new shares*, thereby increasing the total share base and reducing the *percentage ownership* of all existing shareholders — even though their absolute number of shares remains unchanged.

**(i) Founder's Stake Before & After:**

$$
\text{Stake}_{\text{before}} = \frac{12{,}000}{15{,}000} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

New total after issuing 5,000 fresh shares:

$$
N_{\text{new total}} = 15{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 20{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Stake}_{\text{after}} = \frac{12{,}000}{20{,}000} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

*(2 marks — 1 mark each for before & after.)*

**(ii) Dilution:**

$$
\text{Dilution} = 80\% - 60\% = 20\%
$$

The founder's stake fell by **20 percentage points**. *(1 mark)*

**(iii) Amount Raised:**

$$
\text{Funds Raised} = N_{\text{new}} \times \text{Issue Price} = 5{,}000 \times 50 = 2{,}50{,}000
$$

**Amount raised = ₹2,50,000.** *(1 mark)*

*(Bonus conceptual understanding: 2 marks for explaining why dilution matters even when absolute shares are unchanged.)*

#### Part (b) — Rights & Liabilities of Shareholders (7 Marks)

A shareholder is a *part-owner* of the company with specific statutory rights and liabilities codified under the **Companies Act, 2013**.

**Six Key Rights:**

1. **Right to Vote** – Participate in general meetings and vote on resolutions in proportion to shares held.
2. **Right to Dividend** – Receive a proportional share of profits declared by the Board.
3. **Right to Inspect Records** – Access the register of members, annual returns, and financial statements.
4. **Right to Transfer Shares** – Freely sell or transfer shares (subject to articles of association in private companies).
5. **Right to Sue on Behalf of the Company** – File derivative suits if the company's interests are harmed by directors.
6. **Right to Receive Notice & Attend Meetings** – Receive timely notice of AGMs/EGMs and participate.

**Two Key Liabilities:**

1. **Liability Limited to Unpaid Amount** – A shareholder is liable only to the extent of any *unpaid* face value of shares held.
2. **Liability on Winding Up** – Once capital is fully paid, no further liability arises. In the case of partly paid shares, the unpaid balance becomes due.

*(1 mark per right × 6 = 6 marks; 1 mark for any one liability or concluding statement.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing *Stake %* with *Number of Shares*** — A student holding 1,000 shares in a 10,000-share company does NOT hold 1,000%; they hold 10%.
> 2. **Forgetting the *Preference Dividend* deduction in EPS** — Always subtract preference dividend from net profit before dividing by equity shares.
> 3. **Mixing *Authorized* and *Issued* capital** — When a numerical problem says "shares outstanding," treat that as the denominator for stake %.
> 4. **Ignoring units** — EPS and DPS are "per share"; Market Cap is in absolute ₹; Yield is in %. Each carries a half-mark for correct unit mention.
> 5. **Writing generic definitions without parameters** — In "Distinguish" questions, ALWAYS use a table; it earns quicker marks than prose.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Share** = smallest indivisible unit of company capital; **Stake** = % ownership interest.
- ✅ **Two main share types**: Equity (voting, residual) vs Preference (fixed dividend, priority).
- ✅ **Controlling Stake** > 50%; **Super Majority** ≥ 75%; **Blocking Minority** ≥ 25% + 1 share.
- ✅ **Stake % formula:** $N_{\text{owned}} / N_{\text{total}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **EPS formula:** subtract preference dividend from net profit, then divide by equity share count.
- ✅ **P/E ratio** = Market Price / EPS — tells you how much investors pay per ₹1 of earnings.
- ✅ **Dividend Yield** = DPS / Market Price × 100 — income return on share investment.
- ✅ **Market Cap** = Total Shares × Market Price — total market value of the company.
- ✅ **Dilution** = fall in stake % when fresh shares are issued; absolute shares held remain unchanged.
- ✅ **Book Value per Share** = (Total Assets − Total Liabilities − Preference Capital) / Equity Shares.
- ✅ **Authorized > Issued > Subscribed > Paid-up** (in hierarchical order of capital structure).
- ✅ **For Part B numericals**, always show the formula, substitute values, and write the final answer with units.
- ✅ **For Part A distinguish questions**, prefer a markdown table — examiners reward clarity.
- ✅ **Real-world link:** Every startup founder, angel investor, and stock trader uses these calculations daily — mastery here gives you financial literacy for life.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valuation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT743 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP AND SMALL BUSINESS ENTERPRISES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Valuation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Valuation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation** is the analytical process of determining the present economic worth of an asset, a business enterprise, or a liability, expressed in monetary terms, based on identifiable market, income, or asset-based parameters at a specific point in time.

In the context of **Entrepreneurship and Small Business Enterprises (ESBE)**, valuation is the systematic estimation of the **fair market value (FMV)** of a small business, a startup, or a going concern. It is a critical entrepreneurial skill required during:
- **Entry** (purchase of an existing business or franchise)
- **Growth** (raising venture capital, private equity, or debt)
- **Exit** (mergers, acquisitions, succession planning, or IPO)
- **Dispute resolution** (shareholder conflicts, divorce settlements, taxation)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Valuation is covered under *Module 2: Business Plan & Resource Mobilization* in PESFT743. Students are expected to demonstrate the ability to compute the value of a small enterprise using at least three standard methods and to justify the choice of method.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Value (Conceptual Foundation)

Any business, regardless of size, derives its value from one (or a combination) of the following three economic foundations:

| Pillar | What it Measures | Core Question Answered |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Asset-Based Value** | Tangible + Intangible net assets | What is left if the business is liquidated today? |
| **Earnings-Based Value** | Future profit-generating capacity | How much cash will this business throw off in the future? |
| **Market-Based Value** | Comparables in the open market | What are similar businesses selling for? |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Valuing a Used Car"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Buying a Second-Hand Car**
>
> Imagine you are buying a 2018 Maruti Swift from a friend. You will not blindly pay whatever he asks. Instead, you intuitively perform a mini-valuation:
>
> 1. **Asset-Based Thinking:** "What is the metal, tyres, and battery worth if I scrap it?" → Gives a *floor value*.
> 2. **Earnings-Based Thinking:** "This car will save me ₹15,000/month in taxi fares for the next 5 years. So it is worth the present value of those savings." → Gives an *income value*.
> 3. **Market-Based Thinking:** "Let me check OLX — similar 2018 Swifts are selling for ₹4.5 lakhs." → Gives a *market value*.
>
> The **true price** is a negotiated blend of these three perspectives. **Business valuation works exactly the same way** — it is the art and science of blending the three pillars into one defensible number.

## 1.4 Why Valuation Matters for Small Business Entrepreneurs

- **Raising Capital:** Investors (angels, VCs, banks) demand a credible valuation before investing.
- **Strategic Decisions:** Buy, sell, merge, or expand decisions are anchored in valuation.
- **Tax & Legal Compliance:** Wealth tax, gift tax, stamp duty, and succession planning all require a fair valuation report by a *Registered Valuer*.
- **Negotiation Leverage:** A scientifically-derived valuation gives the entrepreneur a data-backed bargaining position.
- **Performance Benchmarking:** Periodic valuation tracks the **entrepreneurial wealth creation** over time.

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Glossary)

- **Going Concern:** A business assumed to operate indefinitely into the future.
- **Fair Market Value (FMV):** The price at which a willing buyer and willing seller, both knowledgeable, would transact.
- **Intrinsic Value:** The "true" worth derived from fundamentals (cash flows, assets), independent of market sentiment.
- **Goodwill:** An intangible asset representing reputation, brand equity, customer loyalty, and superior earnings.
- **Capitalization:** The process of converting a stream of future income into a single present lump-sum value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Valuation Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 expects students to classify and apply the methods under **three master approaches** shown below. Each method answers a different business question.

### A. Asset-Based Methods (Balance Sheet / Net Asset Approach)
These methods look at **what the business owns minus what it owes** on the valuation date.

1. **Book Value Method (Net Asset Method)**
   - Value = Total Assets (at book value) − Total Outside Liabilities
   - Simplest method; suitable for **holding companies** or businesses about to be **liquidated**.

2. **Adjusted Net Asset Method (Replacement / Realizable Value)**
   - Value = Replacement cost of assets (or realizable value) − Liabilities
   - More realistic than book value; used in **professional practice**.

3. **Liquidation Value Method**
   - Value = Forced-sale proceeds of assets − Liabilities
   - Used in **bankruptcy** or **winding-up** scenarios.

### B. Earnings-Based Methods (Income Approach)
These methods value the business by capitalizing its **future profit-earning capacity**.

1. **Capitalization of Average Profits Method (Years' Purchase of Profits)**
   - Treats goodwill as a capital sum equal to a number of years' purchase of average future profits.

2. **Super Profit Method (Capitalization of Super Profit)**
   - Goodwill = Super Profit × Number of Years' Purchase of Super Profit.

3. **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Method**
   - The gold standard: value is the sum of **all future cash flows discounted to present value**.

### C. Market-Based Methods (Relative / Comparison Approach)
- **Comparable Company Analysis (Comps):** Compare valuation multiples (P/E, EV/EBITDA) of similar listed or recently sold private businesses.
- **Comparable Transaction Analysis:** Look at M&A deal prices in the same industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** For small businesses (which are mostly unlisted), the **Earnings-Based methods (DCF + Super Profit)** are the most commonly tested, often combined with the **Goodwill valuation** sub-topic.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the formulas below. They appear in **almost every KTU question paper** on this topic.

### Table 2.1: Master Formula Reference

| Method | Key Formula | Symbol Glossary | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Book Value | $V = TA - TL$ | TA = Total Assets, TL = Total Liabilities | Holding companies |
| Capitalization of Average Profit | $V = AP \times YP$ | AP = Average Profit, YP = Years' Purchase = 100/i | Stable businesses |
| Super Profit Method | $GW = SP \times YP_{SP}$ | GW = Goodwill, SP = Super Profit | Profit > Normal |
| Weighted Average Profit | $W\!AP = \dfrac{\sum (P_i \times W_i)}{\sum W_i}$ | $P_i$ = Year profit, $W_i$ = weight (e.g., 1,2,3) | Trended profits |
| DCF (Single Cash Flow) | $PV = \dfrac{CF}{(1+r)^n}$ | CF = Cash Flow, r = Discount rate, n = year | Future cash value |
| DCF (Annuity - Perpetuity) | $PV = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | C = Annual constant cash flow | Perpetual cash flows |
| Capitalized Value of Business | $CV = \dfrac{AP}{r}$ | AP = Average maintainable profit, r = normal ROI | Income approach |
| Goodwill via Capitalization | $GW = \dfrac{AP}{r} - CA$ | CA = Capital Employed (net assets) | Excess earning power |

### Table 2.2: Critical Supporting Definitions

| Term | Formula / Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Capital Employed (CA)** | Total Assets − Outside Liabilities *(NOT including capital reserve or fictitious assets)* |
| **Normal Profit** | $NP = CA \times \dfrac{r}{100}$ where r = expected rate of return (usually industry average) |
| **Average Profit (AP)** | $\dfrac{\text{Sum of past profits}}{\text{Number of years}}$ (or weighted average) |
| **Super Profit (SP)** | $SP = AP - NP$ (excess of actual over normal) |
| **Years' Purchase (YP)** | $YP = \dfrac{100}{r}$ where r is the expected rate of return |
| **Future Maintainable Profit (FMP)** | Profit expected to continue in the future, adjusted for non-recurring items |

## 2.3 Theoretical Reasoning Behind Each Step

### Why "Years' Purchase" = 100 / r?
If a buyer pays $V$ rupees to receive a perpetual annual profit of $AP$, the implied rate of return is:
$$r = \dfrac{AP}{V} \times 100 \implies V = \dfrac{AP \times 100}{r} = AP \times YP$$

### Why Super Profit exists?
A business earns *super profit* when it has some competitive advantage (location, brand, monopoly, skilled promoter) that lets it earn **above the industry-normal return** on capital employed.

### Why DCF works (Time Value of Money)
A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow because of:
- Inflation eroding purchasing power
- Opportunity cost (could be invested elsewhere)
- Risk of non-receipt

Hence future cash flows are **discounted** back to today.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility for Engineers / Entrepreneurs

- **Startup Founders:** Use DCF or Berkus method to value pre-revenue startups.
- **Family Business Succession:** Asset-based + Super Profit gives a fair inheritance valuation.
- **Franchise Purchase:** Earnings multiple (like 3× EBITDA) is industry standard.
- **Bank Loan Appraisal:** Banks use DCF to assess debt-servicing capacity.
- **M&A:** Comparables + DCF are the two pillars of any deal fairness opinion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Years' Purchase and Capitalized Value

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. Understand the logic, not just the formula.

**Setup:** A business earns an average profit of ₹5,00,000. The normal rate of return in this industry is **10%**. Compute (a) Years' Purchase, (b) Capitalized Value of the business, (c) Goodwill using the Capitalization of Average Profit method, given Capital Employed = ₹30,00,000.

**Step 1: Years of Purchase (YP)**
$$YP = \dfrac{100}{r} = \dfrac{100}{10} = 10 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2: Capitalized Value (CV) of the business**
$$CV = AP \times YP = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 10 = \text{₹50,00,000}$$

**Step 3: Goodwill via Capitalization**
$$GW = CV - CA = 50{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹20,00,000}$$

**Interpretation:** The business is worth ₹50 lakhs; ₹30 lakhs is the "physical" capital and ₹20 lakhs is the intangible premium (Goodwill).

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Super Profit Method (Full Marks Question)

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A firm had the following profits for the last 5 years: ₹50,000; ₹60,000; ₹70,000; ₹80,000; ₹90,000. Capital Employed = ₹8,00,000. Normal Rate of Return = 12%. Compute Goodwill by the **Super Profit Method** using 3 years' purchase of super profit.

**Step 1: Compute Total Profit and Average Profit**
$$\text{Total Profit} = 50{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 70{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 90{,}000 = \text{₹3,50,000}$$

$$AP = \dfrac{3{,}50{,}000}{5} = \text{₹70,000}$$

**Step 2: Compute Normal Profit (the benchmark)**
$$NP = CA \times \dfrac{r}{100} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{12}{100} = \text{₹96,000}$$

**Step 3: Compute Super Profit**
$$SP = AP - NP = 70{,}000 - 96{,}000 = -\text{₹26,000}$$

**Step 4: Re-evaluation (Negative SP case)**

> [!WARNING]
> A negative Super Profit means **no goodwill exists** by this method. The business is actually *under-performing* its industry benchmark. KTU students often ignore the sign and still compute goodwill — losing 2 marks.

In such a case, the student should write:
> "Since SP is negative, Goodwill under the Super Profit Method is **NIL (₹0)**. The business has no goodwill because its actual profits are below the normal industry return."

**Modified Problem (Assume AP = ₹1,20,000 instead):**
$$SP = 1{,}20{,}000 - 96{,}000 = \text{₹24,000}$$
$$GW = SP \times YP = 24{,}000 \times 3 = \text{₹72,000}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Method

**Problem:** A small business expects the following free cash flows (FCF) for the next 4 years: Year 1 = ₹2,00,000; Year 2 = ₹2,50,000; Year 3 = ₹3,00,000; Year 4 = ₹3,50,000. The terminal value at the end of Year 4 is ₹25,00,000. Discount rate (cost of capital) = **12%**. Find the present value (fair value) of the business.

**Step 1: Discount each year's cash flow**
$$PV_1 = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^1} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.1200} = \text{₹1,78,571.43}$$

$$PV_2 = \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{(1.12)^2} = \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.2544} = \text{₹1,99,298.47}$$

$$PV_3 = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^3} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.4049} = \text{₹2,13,553.20}$$

$$PV_4 = \dfrac{3{,}50{,}000}{(1.12)^4} = \dfrac{3{,}50{,}000}{1.5735} = \text{₹2,22,434.38}$$

**Step 2: Discount the Terminal Value (TV)**
$$PV_{TV} = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^4} = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000}{1.5735} = \text{₹15,88,102.64}$$

**Step 3: Sum all present values (Enterprise Value)**
$$EV = PV_1 + PV_2 + PV_3 + PV_4 + PV_{TV}$$

$$EV = 1{,}78{,}571.43 + 1{,}99{,}298.47 + 2{,}13{,}553.20 + 2{,}22{,}434.38 + 15{,}88{,}102.64$$

$$\boxed{EV = \text{₹24,01,960.12}}$$

**Step 4 (Optional): Equity Value**
$$Equity\ Value = EV - Total\ Debt + Cash\ on\ Balance\ Sheet$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always show the discount factors to **4 decimal places** and label each row (Year | FCF | DF | PV). Marks are awarded for clear tabular presentation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation #4 — Weighted Average Profit Method

**Problem:** Profits of a business for 4 years: Year 1 = ₹40,000; Year 2 = ₹50,000; Year 3 = ₹60,000; Year 4 = ₹70,000. Weight the most recent year 4-times and the earliest year 1-time. Compute Goodwill using 3 years' purchase of weighted average profit. Capital Employed = ₹5,00,000; Normal Rate of Return = 10%.

**Step 1: Assign weights**
| Year | Profit (₹) | Weight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 40,000 | 1 |
| 2 | 50,000 | 2 |
| 3 | 60,000 | 3 |
| 4 | 70,000 | 4 |
| **Total** | — | **10** |

**Step 2: Weighted Total**
$$\sum (P_i \times W_i) = (40{,}000 \times 1) + (50{,}000 \times 2) + (60{,}000 \times 3) + (70{,}000 \times 4)$$
$$= 40{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 2{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹6,00{,}000}$$

**Step 3: Weighted Average Profit (WAP)**
$$WAP = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{10} = \text{₹60,000}$$

**Step 4: Normal Profit**
$$NP = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{10}{100} = \text{₹50,000}$$

**Step 5: Super Profit**
$$SP = 60{,}000 - 50{,}000 = \text{₹10,000}$$

**Step 6: Goodwill**
$$GW = SP \times YP = 10{,}000 \times 3 = \text{₹30,000}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Situation in Small Business | Recommended Method | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Service firm with stable cash flows (e.g., salon, clinic) | DCF / Capitalization | Earnings are predictable |
| Asset-heavy manufacturing unit | Adjusted Net Asset Method | Realisable value of machinery matters |
| Loss-making business | Liquidation Value | Continuity is doubtful |
| Brand-driven firm (restaurant, hospital) | Super Profit / Market Multiple | Intangible goodwill is the key driver |
| Pre-revenue startup | Berkus / Scorecard / Cost-to-Replicate | No earnings yet; methods are qualitative |
| Family firm succession | Asset + Goodwill (Average Profit) | Balanced and acceptable to all heirs |
| Bank loan appraisal | DCF + Liquidation | Bank needs both repayment capacity and security cover |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Valuation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Business Valuation Start] --> B{What is the primary value driver?}
    
    B -- Tangible Assets Dominant --> C[Asset-Based Approach]
    B -- Future Earnings Dominant --> D[Earnings-Based Approach]
    B -- Market Comparables Dominant --> E[Market-Based Approach]
    
    C --> C1[Book Value Method]
    C --> C2[Adjusted Net Asset Method]
    C --> C3[Liquidation Value Method]
    
    D --> D1[Capitalization of Average Profit]
    D --> D2[Super Profit Method]
    D --> D3[Discounted Cash Flow DCF]
    D --> D4[Weighted Average Profit Method]
    
    E --> E1[Comparable Company Analysis]
    E --> E2[Comparable Transaction Analysis]
    E --> E3[Industry Multiple Method]
    
    C1 --> F[Sub Total Valuation]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    
    F --> G[Final Business Value Report]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Goodwill Valuation Process — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Historical Profit Data] --> B[Compute Average / Weighted Average Profit]
    B --> C{Choose Method}
    
    C -- Method 1 --> D[Capitalization of Average Profit]
    C -- Method 2 --> E[Super Profit Method]
    C -- Method 3 --> F[Annuity Method]
    
    D --> D1[AP x 100 / r]
    D1 --> G[Goodwill = Capitalized Value - Capital Employed]
    
    E --> E1[Normal Profit = CA x r / 100]
    E1 --> E2[Super Profit = AP - NP]
    E2 --> E3[Goodwill = SP x YP]
    E3 --> G
    
    F --> F1[AP - Normal Remuneration]
    F1 --> F2[Goodwill = Excess x Annuity Factor at r, n years]
    F2 --> G
    
    G --> H[Final Goodwill Figure]
    H --> I[Valuation Report Issued]
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 DCF Valuation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forecast Free Cash Flows Years 1 to N] --> B[Determine Discount Rate WACC]
    B --> C[Apply Discount Factor Formula 1 / 1+r to the power n]
    C --> D[Compute Present Value of Each Year FCF]
    D --> E[Compute Terminal Value at end of Year N]
    E --> F[Discount Terminal Value back to Year 0]
    F --> G[Sum All Present Values]
    G --> H[Enterprise Value EV]
    H --> I[Subtract Net Debt]
    I --> J[Equity Value = Final Business Valuation]
    
    style A fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map — Why Businesses Have Goodwill

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Business Entity] --> B[Tangible Assets]
    A --> C[Intangible Assets]
    
    C --> C1[Brand Equity]
    C --> C2[Customer Loyalty]
    C --> C3[Location Advantage]
    C --> C4[Skilled Promoter Reputation]
    C --> C5[Monopoly / Patent Rights]
    C --> C6[Supplier Relationships]
    
    B --> D[Net Asset Value]
    C1 --> E[Goodwill Premium]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
    C5 --> E
    C6 --> E
    
    D --> F[Total Business Value]
    E --> F
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Valuation. Mention any two methods of valuation of a small business. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
> **Valuation** is the process of determining the present monetary worth of a business enterprise, asset, or liability, based on identifiable economic parameters at a given point in time.
>
> **Two methods of valuation of a small business:**
> 1. **Asset-Based Method (Book Value / Net Asset Method):** Computes value as Total Assets minus Total Outside Liabilities.
> 2. **Earnings-Based Method (Super Profit Method):** Values the business based on its future maintainable profits and capitalizes the super profit.
>
> *(Other acceptable methods: DCF, Capitalization of Average Profit, Market Multiple, Weighted Average Profit.)* **(3 Marks)**

---

### Q2. What is Goodwill? Why is it considered an intangible asset? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
> **Goodwill** is an intangible asset that represents the future economic benefits arising from assets that are not capable of being individually identified or separately recognized, such as brand reputation, customer loyalty, location advantage, and skilled management.
>
> It is considered **intangible** because:
> 1. It cannot be seen or physically touched.
> 2. It cannot be separated from the business and sold independently.
> 3. Its value is derived from the excess of actual earnings over normal earnings, not from any physical asset.
> 4. It appears on the balance sheet only when acquired (e.g., during purchase of a business). **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the different methods of valuation of a small business enterprise. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following details relate to a small firm:
- Average profit of the firm: ₹1,20,000
- Capital Employed: ₹10,00,000
- Normal Rate of Return: 10%

Compute the value of Goodwill by:
1. Capitalization of Average Profit Method
2. Super Profit Method (using 4 years' purchase)

**(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Methods of Valuation (7 Marks):**

A business can be valued using the following three broad approaches:

**1. Asset-Based Approach (3 sub-methods):**
- **Book Value Method:** $V = \text{Total Assets} - \text{Total Liabilities}$. Simple but ignores current market realities.
- **Adjusted Net Asset Method:** Replaces book values with current replacement or realizable values. More realistic.
- **Liquidation Value Method:** Assumes assets are sold under distress; relevant in winding-up scenarios.

**2. Earnings-Based Approach (Income Approach):**
- **Capitalization of Average Profits:** Business value = AP × (100/r).
- **Super Profit Method:** Goodwill = (AP − Normal Profit) × Years' Purchase.
- **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF):** Present value of all future free cash flows + discounted terminal value. Theoretically the most rigorous method.
- **Weighted Average Profit Method:** Gives higher weight to recent years' profits to reflect current earning capacity.

**3. Market-Based Approach (Relative Valuation):**
- **Comparable Company Analysis (Comps):** Uses multiples like P/E, EV/EBITDA from listed peers.
- **Comparable Transaction Analysis:** Studies recent M&A deal prices in the same sector.
- **Industry Multiple Method:** Applies a sector average multiple to the firm's earnings metric.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Years' Purchase (YP)**
$$YP = \dfrac{100}{r} = \dfrac{100}{10} = 10 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2: Capitalization of Average Profit Method**

Capitalized Value of Business:
$$CV = AP \times YP = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 10 = \text{₹12,00{,}000}$$

Goodwill:
$$GW = CV - CA = 12{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹2,00{,}000}$$

**Step 3: Super Profit Method**

Normal Profit:
$$NP = CA \times \dfrac{r}{100} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{10}{100} = \text{₹1,00{,}000}$$

Super Profit:
$$SP = AP - NP = 1{,}20{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹20,000}$$

Goodwill (with 4 years' purchase):
$$GW = SP \times 4 = 20{,}000 \times 4 = \text{₹80,000}$$

**Step 4: Final Answer Summary**
| Method | Goodwill (₹) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Capitalization of Average Profit | 2,00,000 |
| Super Profit (4 YP) | 80,000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **The difference is acceptable** because the two methods rely on different economic logic. KTU may also ask students to justify the method preferred. **(Total 14 Marks)**

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** What is Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Method? Explain its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small business expects free cash flows of ₹3,00,000 in Year 1, ₹3,50,000 in Year 2, and ₹4,00,000 in Year 3. The expected terminal value at the end of Year 3 is ₹30,00,000. Using a discount rate of 10%, compute the value of the business using the DCF method. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — DCF Method: Theory (7 Marks):**

> **Definition:** The **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Method** is a valuation technique that estimates the value of a business by projecting its future free cash flows and discounting them back to the present using a suitable discount rate (cost of capital, WACC, or required rate of return).

**Mathematical Form:**
$$V = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{FCF_t}{(1+r)^t} + \dfrac{TV}{(1+r)^n}$$

where $V$ = Business Value, $FCF_t$ = Free Cash Flow in year t, $r$ = Discount rate, $TV$ = Terminal Value, $n$ = forecast horizon.

**Advantages:**
1. Theoretically the most rigorous method — based on fundamental cash generation capacity.
2. Incorporates the **Time Value of Money**.
3. Allows sensitivity analysis by varying discount rate or growth assumptions.
4. Suitable for businesses with identifiable future cash flows.
5. Aligned with shareholder value creation logic.

**Limitations:**
1. Highly sensitive to the assumed discount rate and growth projections.
2. Difficult to apply to early-stage startups with unpredictable cash flows.
3. Requires accurate forecasts — estimation error compounds over years.
4. Choice of terminal value can swing the final valuation dramatically.
5. Hard to value intangibles like brand and goodwill using only DCF.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Discount Factors**
$$DF_1 = \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^1} = 0.9091$$
$$DF_2 = \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^2} = 0.8264$$
$$DF_3 = \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^3} = 0.7513$$

**Step 2: Compute Present Value of Each Year's FCF**
$$PV_1 = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9091 = \text{₹2,72,730}$$
$$PV_2 = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.8264 = \text{₹2,89,240}$$
$$PV_3 = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7513 = \text{₹3,00,520}$$

**Step 3: Present Value of Terminal Value**
$$PV_{TV} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7513 = \text{₹22,53,900}$$

**Step 4: Total Enterprise Value**
$$EV = 2{,}72{,}730 + 2{,}89{,}240 + 3{,}00{,}520 + 22{,}53{,}900 = \text{₹31,16,390}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Business Value (EV)} = \text{₹31,16,390} \approx \text{₹31.16 Lakhs}}$$

**Step 5: Tabular Presentation (Marks Awarded)**
| Year | FCF (₹) | Discount Factor | Present Value (₹) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 3,00,000 | 0.9091 | 2,72,730 |
| 2 | 3,50,000 | 0.8264 | 2,89,240 |
| 3 | 4,00,000 | 0.7513 | 3,00,520 |
| Terminal | 30,00,000 | 0.7513 | 22,53,900 |
| **Total** | — | — | **31,16,390** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of Super Profit:** Always check if AP > NP before computing goodwill. A negative SP = NIL goodwill.
> 2. **Discount Factors:** Round to 4 decimal places; do not truncate early. Marks are deducted for sloppy rounding.
> 3. **Capital Employed:** Exclude fictitious assets (preliminary expenses, accumulated losses) and non-business investments.
> 4. **Weighting Convention:** If not specified, assume the **most recent year has the highest weight** (e.g., 1, 2, 3, 4 for 4 years).
> 5. **Goodwill vs. Business Value:** Students often confuse Goodwill with Total Business Value. Goodwill is only the **excess over capital employed**; it is one component of the total valuation.
> 6. **Years' Purchase Unit:** Always state "years" (e.g., 10 years' purchase) — writing only the number loses 0.5 marks.
> 7. **Method Selection:** If a question asks "which method is most suitable", justify using the **nature of business** (asset-heavy vs. service vs. loss-making).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Valuation (PESFT743 — Module 2):**

- **Valuation = Estimation of fair market worth** of a business at a point in time.
- **Three master approaches:** Asset-Based, Earnings-Based (Income), Market-Based (Relative).
- **Book Value Method:** $V = TA - TL$. Simplest, used in liquidation.
- **Adjusted Net Asset Method:** Replaces book values with current realizable or replacement values.
- **Capital Employed (CA):** Net assets used in the business — exclude fictitious assets.
- **Average Profit (AP):** Simple or weighted average of past 3–5 years' profits.
- **Weighted Average:** Give higher weights to recent years (1, 2, 3, 4 for 4 years).
- **Normal Profit (NP):** $NP = CA \times r/100$ — the industry-benchmark return.
- **Super Profit (SP):** $SP = AP - NP$. If negative, **Goodwill = NIL**.
- **Years' Purchase (YP):** $YP = 100/r$. Always expressed in "years".
- **Goodwill (Super Profit Method):** $GW = SP \times YP$.
- **Goodwill (Capitalization Method):** $GW = (AP \times YP) - CA$.
- **DCF Formula:** $PV = FCF / (1+r)^n$ for each year; sum all PVs; add discounted terminal value.
- **Terminal Value:** Estimated value of business at the end of forecast horizon — usually 70–80% of total DCF value.
- **Discount Rate:** The required rate of return, often the firm's **WACC** (Weighted Average Cost of Capital).
- **Market Multiple Methods:** P/E (Price-to-Earnings), EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales — used for comparables.
- **Goodwill is intangible:** Cannot be separated from the business; appears on balance sheet only on acquisition.
- **Factors generating Goodwill:** Brand, location, monopoly, customer loyalty, skilled promoter, patents, supplier relations.
- **Method selection logic:**
  - Loss-making → Liquidation Value
  - Stable, profitable → DCF or Capitalization
  - Brand-driven → Super Profit
  - Asset-heavy → Adjusted Net Asset
  - Pre-revenue startup → Berkus / Cost-to-Replicate
- **Valuation is both an ART (judgment) and a SCIENCE (formulas).** A credible valuation report blends both.
- **Final valuation report should reconcile at least two methods** and present a defensible range, not a single number.
- **Memorize the 5 key formulas:** Book Value, AP × YP, Super Profit × YP, DCF, Capitalization method for Goodwill.
- **Always show assumptions** (discount rate, growth rate, terminal value method) — KTU values clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finance Creation (Investors/Financers)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT743 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP AND SMALL BUSINESS ENTERPRISES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Finance Creation (Investors/Financers)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Finance Creation (Investors/Financiers)** refers to the strategic process by which an entrepreneur identifies, approaches, structures, and secures external capital from equity investors (those who buy ownership in the business) and debt financiers (those who lend money to the business) to fund the startup, growth, or expansion of a Small Business Enterprise (SBE).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Finance Creation* is the **bridge between an entrepreneurial idea and its commercial execution**. It is not merely "borrowing money" — it is the disciplined engineering of capital structures that align the risk-return expectations of external capital providers with the strategic objectives of the entrepreneur.

### Taxonomy of Finance Providers

| Category | Equity Investors | Debt Financiers |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Exposure** | Bear business risk; lose investment if firm fails | Bear credit risk; expect repayment with interest |
| **Return Mechanism** | Capital gains + dividends | Fixed interest + principal repayment |
| **Control Rights** | Receive equity stake, voting rights, board seats | No ownership; secured by collateral or covenants |
| **Time Horizon** | Medium to long-term (3–10 years) | Short to long-term (1–10 years) |
| **Typical Examples** | Angel Investors, Venture Capitalists, Private Equity, Crowdfunding (equity-based) | Banks, NBFCs, Microfinance Institutions, Government Schemes, Trade Credit |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PESFT743 emphasizes that an entrepreneur must understand *who gives money, why they give it, what they expect in return, and how to pitch effectively*. This is the core of "Finance Creation."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Finance Creation like raising a child.** An entrepreneur is the **parent** with a brilliant baby (the business idea). **Investors** are like **adoptive parents** who take a share of the child's future — they want the child to grow into a successful adult because their share of the child's achievements will be valuable. **Financiers (lenders)** are like **uncles who give pocket money** — they want their money back with a little extra (interest), but they don't want to know the child's daily struggles. The entrepreneur must learn to "ask" from the right people, the right way, at the right time.

### Key Intuitions to Remember

- **Investors buy a piece of your future** — they trade money today for a share of tomorrow's profits.
- **Financiers rent you their money** — they expect it back with a rental fee (interest).
- **The cost of capital differs:** Equity is typically more expensive in the long run (you give up ownership), but flexible in cash flow. Debt is cheaper in nominal terms but rigid (must be repaid regardless of business performance).
- **Bootstrapping** (using personal savings, family money, or early customer revenue) is the seed — but **investors/financiers** are the water and sunlight that allow scaling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** The **Rule of Thumb** in Indian startup finance is the **"3F + 3V"** framework — **Family, Friends, Fools** (early informal investors) followed by **Venture Capitalists, VCs, and VCs** (wait, that's three VCs!). The correct standard framework is **3F (Family, Friends, Fools) → Angel Investors → Venture Capital → Private Equity/IPO**, representing the typical financing lifecycle stages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Investors — Theoretical Breakdown

### A. Angel Investors
**Definition:** High-net-worth individuals (HNIs) who invest their personal funds in early-stage startups, typically in exchange for equity.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Angel investors usually invest during the **Seed or Pre-Seed stage**.
2. Ticket size: **₹25 lakh to ₹5 crore** in India (varies by city and sector).
3. Beyond money, they provide **mentorship, network access, and credibility**.
4. They accept **higher risk** in exchange for **higher equity stakes (typically 10%–25%)**.
5. They invest through **SAFE notes, convertible debentures, or direct equity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Platform Examples in India:** Indian Angel Network (IAN), LetsVenture, Mumbai Angels, Hyderabad Angels.

### B. Venture Capitalists (VCs)
**Definition:** Professional fund managers who pool capital from institutional investors (pension funds, endowments, sovereign wealth funds) and invest in high-growth potential startups.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. VCs invest in **Series A, B, C, and beyond** — i.e., post-revenue or post-traction stages.
2. Ticket size: **₹5 crore to ₹500+ crore** depending on stage and fund size.
3. They demand **board seats, veto rights, anti-dilution clauses, and liquidation preferences**.
4. They follow a **fund life cycle** — typically 10 years (5 years investing, 5 years harvesting/exiting).
5. Exit mechanisms: **IPO, strategic acquisition, secondary sale, or buyback**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The "**Power Law**" of venture capital — VCs expect that **1 out of 10 investments** will return **10x**, **2 will return 3–5x**, and the rest will fail or return 1x. This is why VCs demand outsized returns on winners.

### C. Private Equity (PE) Firms
**Definition:** Investors who acquire significant equity stakes in **mature, profitable companies** for control, restructuring, or expansion.

**Key Difference from VC:**
- VC = early-stage, minority stake, growth-focused.
- PE = late-stage, often majority/control stake, profitability-focused, leveraged buyouts (LBOs).

### D. Equity-Based Crowdfunding
**Definition:** A platform-based mechanism where many small investors contribute small amounts to collectively fund a startup in exchange for equity.

**Examples:** Ketto (now donation-focused), FundFunder, Tyke (India), Seedrs, Crowdcube (UK).

### E. Strategic Investors / Corporate Venturing
**Definition:** Established corporations that invest in startups for **strategic synergies** (technology access, market entry, innovation pipeline).

**Examples:** Google Ventures, Intel Capital, Reliance Ventures, Tata Sons investments in startups.

### F. Government and Institutional Investors
- **SIDBI** (Small Industries Development Bank of India) — funds VCs and directly lends to MSMEs.
- **NABARD** — rural and agri-entrepreneurship finance.
- **MUDRA Yojana** — microfinance for non-corporate small businesses (Shishu: up to ₹50,000; Kishore: ₹50,000–5 lakh; Tarun: ₹5–10 lakh).
- **Stand-Up India** — SC/ST and women entrepreneurs.
- **Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS)** — government-backed seed funding up to ₹20 crore per startup.

## 2.2 Types of Financiers (Debt Providers) — Theoretical Breakdown

### A. Commercial Banks
**Definition:** The most common source of debt finance for small businesses. Offer term loans, working capital loans, cash credit, and overdraft facilities.

**Key Loan Products for SBEs:**
- **Term Loan** — for fixed asset purchase (machinery, land, building).
- **Cash Credit / Overdraft** — for working capital needs.
- **Letter of Credit / Bank Guarantee** — for trade finance.
- **Mudra Loans** — disbursed via PSU banks under MUDRA scheme.

### B. Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs)
**Definition:** Institutions registered under the Companies Act that provide loan services but cannot accept demand deposits like banks.

**Examples:** Bajaj Finance, HDFC Ltd (pre-merger), Muthoot Finance, Lendingkart.
**Advantage over Banks:** Faster disbursement, flexible eligibility, willing to fund unbanked or thin-file borrowers.

### C. Microfinance Institutions (MFIs)
**Definition:** Provide small loans (microcredit) to low-income entrepreneurs, typically without collateral, using **joint liability group (JLG)** models.

**Examples:** SKS Microfinance (now Bharat Microfinance), Bandhan Bank, CreditAccess Grameen.
**KTU Insight:** MFIs are critical for **inclusive entrepreneurship** in rural India.

### D. Trade Credit and Supplier Financing
**Definition:** Allowing buyers to purchase goods/services on credit (e.g., 30/60/90 days) — a form of spontaneous short-term finance.

### E. Government-Backed Schemes
| Scheme | Target | Funding Range |
|---|---|---|
| **MUDRA Yojana** | Non-corporate small businesses | Up to ₹10 lakh |
| **Stand-Up India** | SC/ST, Women | ₹10 lakh – ₹1 crore |
| **CGTMSE** (Credit Guarantee Fund Trust) | MSMEs | Up to ₹5 crore (collateral-free) |
| **PMEGP** (Prime Minister's Employment Generation Programme) | Rural/unemployed youth | ₹10 lakh – ₹25 lakh |
| **Startup India Seed Fund** | DPIIT-recognized startups | Up to ₹20 crore (via incubators) |

### F. Factoring and Forfaiting
**Definition:** Selling accounts receivable (invoices) to a third party (factor) at a discount to get immediate cash. Common in B2B small businesses.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet (Investor vs Financier)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most exam-relevant comparative table. Memorize it.**

| Parameter | Equity Investor | Debt Financier |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal Status** | Part-owner (shareholder) | Creditor (lender) |
| **Risk-Reward** | High risk, high return | Low risk, fixed return |
| **Decision Speed** | Slow (due diligence heavy) | Moderate to fast (collateral-based) |
| **Dilution of Control** | Yes (loses ownership %) | No (ownership unchanged) |
| **Obligation in Loss** | Shares the loss; no guaranteed return | Still must be repaid (unsecured = risk) |
| **Tax Treatment** | Dividends taxed; capital gains taxed | Interest expense tax-deductible for firm |
| **Exit Strategy** | IPO, acquisition, secondary sale | Bullet repayment, EMI, scheduled maturity |
| **Relationship Style** | Long-term partnership | Transactional |
| **For SBE, Best When** | High-growth, scalable model | Stable cash flows, asset-heavy |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

In the Indian entrepreneurial ecosystem, the **choice between investor and financier is strategic**, not financial. A **kirana store owner** in Kerala seeking to scale needs debt (Mudra loan or bank overdraft). A **tech startup founder** in Bangalore building an AI SaaS platform needs equity (angel + VC) because the business has negative cash flows for years and needs to reinvest everything. Misaligned capital structure is one of the top 3 reasons small businesses fail — making *Finance Creation* a core entrepreneurial competency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Framework — The Finance Creation Process

## 3.1 The Investor Funnel (Equity Path)

This is the **standard KTU 3-stage framework** for how an entrepreneur approaches equity investors.

### Stage 1: Pre-Pitch Preparation

**Step 1.1: Develop a Business Plan**
- Problem statement, solution, target market, business model.
- Financial projections: 3–5 year P&L, balance sheet, cash flow.

**Step 1.2: Build the Pitch Deck (10–12 slides)**
The KTU-recognized **"10-Slide Standard Pitch Deck"** structure:
1. Title (company, logo, founder name, one-line tagline).
2. Problem (pain point, market size).
3. Solution (your product/service).
4. Market Opportunity (TAM, SAM, SOM).
5. Business Model (how you make money).
6. Traction (users, revenue, growth metrics).
7. Competition (SWOT or 2x2 matrix).
8. Team (founders, advisors).
9. Financials (3-year projection, key metrics).
10. The Ask (how much, what for, what you'll give).

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Each slide answers one fundamental question. Slide 2 says "Why this matters." Slide 5 says "Why you can win." Slide 10 says "Why I should invest now."

### Stage 2: Investor Identification and Approach

**Step 2.1: Build a Target Investor List**
- Categorize by stage (seed, Series A), sector (fintech, agritech), geography (India, global), and check size.

**Step 2.2: Get Warm Introductions**
- 80%+ of VC funding comes through **warm intros**, not cold emails. Use LinkedIn, accelerators, alumni networks, pitch events.

**Step 2.3: Send the "One-Liner + Deck" Email**
- Subject: Clear, with company name and stage.
- Body: 3-sentence pitch + deck attached.
- Follow-up after 3–5 days.

### Stage 3: Diligence, Term Sheet, and Closing

**Step 3.1: Due Diligence (DD)**
The investor verifies everything. Types:
- **Business DD** — customer interviews, market validation.
- **Financial DD** — audit financials, projection sanity.
- **Legal DD** — incorporation, IP, contracts, pending litigation.
- **Technical DD** — for deep-tech startups.

**Step 3.2: Term Sheet Negotiation**
A non-binding agreement outlining the investment terms. Key clauses:

| Clause | What It Means | Entrepreneur's Lens |
|---|---|---|
| **Valuation** | Pre-money vs post-money | Higher = less dilution |
| **Equity Stake** | % ownership given | Lower = more retained control |
| **Liquidation Preference** | Who gets paid first on exit | 1x non-participating is fair |
| **Anti-Dilution** | Protection if valuation drops in next round | Avoid "full ratchet" |
| **Board Composition** | Investor gets a seat | Negotiate 1 founder-majority seat |
| **Vesting** | Founder shares vest over time | Standard 4-year, 1-year cliff |
| **Drag-Along** | Investor can force sale | Caution — can be exploited |
| **Pro-Rata Rights** | Right to invest in next round | Generally acceptable |

**Step 3.3: Shareholders' Agreement (SHA) and Share Purchase Agreement (SPA)**
Legal documents binding the deal. Always engage a startup-experienced lawyer.

**Step 3.4: Wire Transfer and Cap Table Update**
Money hits the bank. Cap table (capitalization table) is updated to reflect new ownership structure.

## 3.2 The Financier Approach (Debt Path)

### Step 1: Identify the Right Financier

$$ \text{Choose Financier} = f(\text{Loan Size}, \text{Collateral Available}, \text{Credit Score}, \text{Industry}) $$

Where:
- Small ticket, no collateral → MUDRA, NBFC, MFI.
- Medium ticket, asset available → Bank term loan.
- Working capital intensive → Cash Credit / Overdraft.
- Export business → Letter of Credit / Pre-shipment credit.

### Step 2: Documentation
Common documents required by Indian banks for SBE loans:
- Business registration certificate (Udyam/MSME registration).
- PAN, Aadhaar of proprietor/partners/directors.
- Last 2–3 years ITR and audited financials.
- Bank statements (last 12 months).
- Project report / business plan.
- Collateral documents (property, FD, machinery).
- GST registration and returns.

### Step 3: Key Debt Metrics (Exam-Important)

$$ \text{Debt-to-Equity Ratio} = \frac{\text{Total Liabilities}}{\text{Owner's Equity}} $$

$$ \text{Interest Coverage Ratio (ICR)} = \frac{\text{EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Tax)}}{\text{Interest Expense}} $$

$$ \text{DSCR (Debt Service Coverage Ratio)} = \frac{\text{Net Operating Income}}{\text{Total Debt Service (Principal + Interest)}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Banks typically require:** ICR ≥ 2.0, DSCR ≥ 1.5, and D/E ≤ 2:1 (varies by sector). Below these, the loan is likely rejected or priced at a higher interest rate.

### Step 4: Loan Repayment Structure

Two standard structures:

**A. Equal Monthly Installments (EMI) — Reducing Balance**

$$ \text{EMI} = \frac{P \cdot r \cdot (1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1} $$

Where:
- $P$ = Principal loan amount.
- $r$ = Monthly interest rate (annual rate ÷ 12).
- $n$ = Number of monthly installments (tenure in months).

**B. Equal Principal Installments** — Equal principal, declining interest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the same as those used in time-value-of-money calculations in **Engineering Economics**, making this topic relevant for inter-disciplinary understanding.

## 3.3 Valuation Basics for Entrepreneurial Finance

When an investor offers to invest, the entrepreneur must know the **valuation** of the company.

### Pre-Money vs Post-Money Valuation

$$ V_{\text{pre}} = \text{Company valuation before investment} $$

$$ V_{\text{post}} = V_{\text{pre}} + \text{Investment Amount} $$

$$ \text{Investor's Equity \%} = \frac{\text{Investment Amount}}{V_{\text{post}}} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{Founder's Dilution} = \frac{\text{Investment Amount}}{V_{\text{post}}} $$

**Worked Example:**

Suppose an entrepreneur values her startup at **₹4 crore pre-money**. An angel investor offers **₹1 crore**.

Step 1: Post-money valuation.

$$ V_{\text{post}} = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ crore} $$

Step 2: Investor's equity stake.

$$ \text{Equity \%} = \frac{1}{5} \times 100 = 20\% $$

Step 3: Founder's retained ownership (was 100%, now).

$$ 100\% - 20\% = 80\% $$

> [!TIP]
> **The same ₹1 crore investment at a higher pre-money valuation (e.g., ₹9 crore) would give only 10% equity.** This is why valuation is the single most negotiated number in any term sheet.

## 3.4 Common Valuation Methods (For Exam Reference)

| Method | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Berkus Method** | Assigns ₹X lakh to each of 5 risk factors (idea, team, prototype, strategic relationships, product rollout) | Pre-revenue, idea-stage |
| **Scorecard Method** | Compare to similar funded startups, adjust for team, market, etc. | Early-stage with comparables |
| **Risk Factor Summation** | Base valuation + adjustment for 12 risk factors | Pre-revenue startups |
| **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** | Sum of PV of future free cash flows | Mature, revenue-generating SBEs |
| **Comparables / Multiples** | EBITDA × industry multiple, or Revenue × multiple | Growth-stage, sector with benchmarks |
| **Venture Capital Method** | $$V_{\text{pre}} = \frac{V_{\text{exit}} \times \text{Target ROI}}{(1+r)^n}$$ | VC's perspective for ROI calculation |

The **VC Method** formula in detail:

$$ V_{\text{pre}} = \frac{V_{\text{exit}} \times \text{Target ROI}}{(1+r)^n} - \text{Future Investment Required} $$

Where:
- $V_{\text{exit}}$ = Expected exit value (e.g., via acquisition or IPO in Year $n$).
- Target ROI = e.g., 10x for early-stage VCs.
- $r$ = Annual discount rate (typically 50%–70% for early-stage).
- $n$ = Years to exit (typically 5–7).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Discount Rate" in VC is not the same as bank interest rates.** A VC uses 50%–70% because the risk of total loss is high; this is a reflection of the **risk-return trade-off** in entrepreneurial finance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Finance Creation Ecosystem (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Entrepreneur with Business Idea] --> B[Bootstrapping - 3F Stage]
    B --> C{Need External Capital?}
    C -->|Yes - For Growth| D{Meets Debt Criteria?}
    C -->|No| B
    D -->|Yes - Stable Cash Flow| E[Debt Financiers Path]
    D -->|No - High Growth, Unprofitable| F[Equity Investors Path]
    
    E --> E1[Bank Term Loan]
    E --> E2[MUDRA Loan]
    E --> E3[NBFC / MFI]
    E --> E4[Government Schemes - CGTMSE etc]
    
    F --> F1[Angel Investor - Seed Stage]
    F --> F2[Venture Capital - Series A B C]
    F --> F3[Private Equity - Late Stage]
    F --> F4[Equity Crowdfunding]
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    
    F1 --> G[Due Diligence Phase]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Term Sheet Negotiation]
    H --> I[Share Purchase Agreement]
    I --> J[Capital Infusion and Cap Table Update]
    
    E1 --> K[EMI Repayment Schedule]
    E2 --> K
    E3 --> K
    K --> L[Loan Closure]
```

## 4.2 The 10-Slide Pitch Deck Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Slide 1: Title] --> P2[Slide 2: Problem]
    P2 --> P3[Slide 3: Solution]
    P3 --> P4[Slide 4: Market Size - TAM SAM SOM]
    P4 --> P5[Slide 5: Business Model]
    P5 --> P6[Slide 6: Traction and Metrics]
    P6 --> P7[Slide 7: Competition - SWOT]
    P7 --> P8[Slide 8: Team]
    P8 --> P9[Slide 9: Financials - 3 Year Projection]
    P9 --> P10[Slide 10: The Ask - Amount Use Exit Timeline]
```

## 4.3 Term Sheet Negotiation Power Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Term Sheet Received] --> T1{Valuation Acceptable?}
    T1 -->|No| T1a[Counter with comparables and traction data]
    T1 -->|Yes| T2{Liquidation Preference Fair?}
    T2 -->|No| T2a[Negotiate to 1x non-participating]
    T2 -->|Yes| T3{Board Composition OK?}
    T3 -->|No| T3a[Negotiate founder-majority seat]
    T3 -->|Yes| T4{Anti-Dilution Acceptable?}
    T4 -->|No| T4a[Avoid full ratchet, prefer weighted average]
    T4 -->|Yes| T5[Vesting and Drag-Along Review]
    T5 --> T6[Engage Startup Lawyer]
    T6 --> T7[Sign SHA and SPA]
    T7 --> T8[Wire Transfer and Cap Table Update]
```

## 4.4 Investor Lifecycle Stages (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Idea Stage] -->|3F Money| B[Seed Stage]
    B -->|Angel - 25L to 5Cr| C[Pre-Series A]
    C -->|Pre-Seed Funds and Accelerators| D[Series A]
    D -->|VC - 5Cr to 50Cr| E[Series B]
    E -->|VC - 50Cr to 200Cr| F[Series C and Beyond]
    F -->|Late Stage VC| G[Pre-IPO]
    G -->|PE / Pre-IPO Funds| H[IPO or Acquisition Exit]
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#FFD580
    style C fill:#FFC966
    style D fill:#FFBD4C
    style E fill:#FFB133
    style F fill:#FFA519
    style G fill:#FF9900
    style H fill:#CC7A00
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Interpretation:** This diagram represents the **financial lifecycle** of a typical high-growth startup. At each transition, the source of capital changes — moving from informal (3F), to formal equity (Angel), to institutional (VC), and finally to public markets (IPO). The entrepreneur's *finance creation* strategy must evolve in lockstep with these stages.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between an equity investor and a debt financier in the context of small business finance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Equity Investor | Debt Financier |
|---|---|---|
| **Role** | Owner / Shareholder | Creditor / Lender |
| **Return** | Dividends, capital gains | Fixed interest |
| **Risk** | Bears business risk | Bears credit risk |
| **Control** | Voting rights, board seats | No control rights |
| **Repayment** | Not mandatory | Mandatory with interest |

*Equity investors share in the upside of business growth while debt financiers are contractually entitled to fixed repayments regardless of business performance.* **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is an angel investor? How does an angel investor differ from a venture capitalist?**

**Model Answer:**

> An **angel investor** is a high-net-worth individual who invests personal funds in early-stage startups, typically providing seed capital along with mentorship.

> An **angel investor differs from a venture capitalist** in three key ways: (1) **Source of capital** — angel uses personal wealth, VC uses pooled institutional money. (2) **Investment size** — angel invests ₹25 lakh to ₹5 crore; VC invests ₹5 crore to ₹500+ crore. (3) **Stage** — angel invests at seed/idea stage; VC invests at Series A and beyond when the startup has proven traction. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"Finance Creation is the strategic engineering of capital structures for an entrepreneurial venture." Discuss the various sources of finance available to a small business enterprise, distinguishing between equity investors and debt financiers. (14 marks)**

**Sub-part (a): Equity Investors and Their Role — 7 marks**

**Model Answer:**

Equity investors provide capital in exchange for ownership stakes in the business. For a small business enterprise, the major types of equity investors are:

**1. Angel Investors (2 marks)**
Angel investors are high-net-worth individuals who invest personal funds in early-stage startups. They typically invest between ₹25 lakh and ₹5 crore. Beyond capital, they provide mentorship, network access, and strategic guidance. Examples in India include the Indian Angel Network and Mumbai Angels.

**2. Venture Capitalists (2 marks)**
Venture Capital (VC) funds are professional investment firms that pool capital from institutional investors. They invest in startups with high-growth potential, typically at Series A and beyond. They expect high returns (often 10x) within 5–7 years and exit through IPOs or acquisitions. VCs often take board seats and demand protective provisions in term sheets.

**3. Private Equity and Strategic Investors (2 marks)**
Private Equity (PE) firms acquire significant equity stakes in mature companies, often for control or restructuring. Strategic investors are corporations that invest for synergies such as technology access or market expansion. Examples include Google Ventures, Intel Capital, and Tata Capital.

**4. Equity Crowdfunding and Government Schemes (1 mark)**
Platforms like LetsVenture allow many small investors to collectively fund a startup. Government schemes like the Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS) provide seed capital of up to ₹20 crore per startup through approved incubators.

**Sub-part (b): Debt Financiers and Their Mechanisms — 7 marks**

**Model Answer:**

Debt financiers lend money to the business with a contractual obligation to repay the principal with interest. For a small business, the major sources are:

**1. Commercial Banks (2 marks)**
Banks are the most common source of debt for SBEs. They offer term loans for fixed assets, cash credit for working capital, and overdraft facilities. Loans are disbursed against collateral and require strong creditworthiness. Key ratios like Interest Coverage Ratio (≥ 2.0) and DSCR (≥ 1.5) are evaluated.

**2. Non-Banking Financial Companies — NBFCs (2 marks)**
NBFCs like Bajaj Finance and Lendingkart provide faster, more flexible loans than banks, often to thin-file or unbanked borrowers. They are critical for small businesses that do not meet traditional bank eligibility criteria.

**3. Microfinance Institutions — MFIs (1.5 marks)**
MFIs like Bandhan provide microloans (typically ₹10,000 to ₹1 lakh) to micro-entrepreneurs, often using joint liability group (JLG) models. They are crucial for financial inclusion in rural India.

**4. Government Schemes (1.5 marks)**
Schemes like **MUDRA Yojana** (up to ₹10 lakh), **CGTMSE** (collateral-free loans up to ₹5 crore), **Stand-Up India** (₹10 lakh to ₹1 crore for SC/ST and women), and **PMEGP** (₹10–25 lakh for self-employment) provide subsidized or guaranteed debt to small businesses.

**Concluding Synthesis:**
The choice between equity and debt depends on the business's growth profile, cash flow stability, and risk appetite. Equity suits high-growth, scalable ventures with negative early cash flows, while debt suits stable, asset-heavy businesses with predictable revenues. The optimal capital structure is one that minimizes the weighted average cost of capital (WACC) while preserving strategic control.

---

### QUESTION B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Prepare a detailed note on the process of finance creation through equity investors. Explain the structure of a pitch deck, key clauses in a term sheet, and the due diligence process. (14 marks)**

**Sub-part (a): The Pitch Deck and Investor Approach Process — 7 marks**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Pre-Pitch Preparation (2 marks)**
Before approaching investors, the entrepreneur must prepare a comprehensive business plan with 3–5 year financial projections and a structured pitch deck. The pitch deck should follow the standard 10-slide format: (1) Title, (2) Problem, (3) Solution, (4) Market Size (TAM/SAM/SOM), (5) Business Model, (6) Traction, (7) Competition, (8) Team, (9) Financials, (10) The Ask.

**2. Identifying the Right Investors (2 marks)**
Investors should be categorized by stage (seed, Series A), sector focus, geography, and typical check size. The entrepreneur should build a target list of 50–100 investors, then prioritize the top 20 based on fit. Warm introductions through accelerators, alumni networks, and LinkedIn are far more effective than cold emails.

**3. The Pitch Meeting (2 marks)**
The first meeting is typically a 30-minute pitch followed by Q&A. Successful pitches tell a story: the founder's journey, the problem being solved, the scale of the opportunity, the unique solution, and the path to returns. The entrepreneur must demonstrate **credibility, market insight, and coachability**.

**4. Follow-Up and Term Sheet (1 mark)**
If interested, the investor issues a term sheet. Multiple term sheets create competitive tension and improve valuation.

**Sub-part (b): Due Diligence and Term Sheet Structure — 7 marks**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Due Diligence Process (3 marks)**
Once a term sheet is signed, the investor conducts due diligence to verify the entrepreneur's claims. This includes:
- **Business DD** — customer interviews, market validation, product reviews.
- **Financial DD** — audit of financials, projection assumptions, unit economics.
- **Legal DD** — incorporation documents, IP assignments, contracts, pending litigation.
- **Technical DD** — for deep-tech startups, an assessment of the technology stack and IP.

The entrepreneur must be prepared with a **data room** — a secure online repository of all relevant documents.

**2. Key Term Sheet Clauses (3 marks)**
A term sheet is a non-binding agreement that outlines the deal structure. Key clauses include:
- **Valuation and Equity** — pre-money vs post-money, investor's stake, founder's dilution.
- **Liquidation Preference** — 1x non-participating is the standard fair term.
- **Anti-Dilution** — protects investor if valuation drops in next round (weighted average preferred over full ratchet).
- **Board Composition** — investors typically seek one seat; entrepreneurs should retain founder-majority.
- **Vesting** — founder shares vest over 4 years with a 1-year cliff.
- **Pro-Rata Rights** — investor's right to participate in future rounds.

**3. Closing the Deal (1 mark)**
After DD, the lawyers draft the **Shareholders' Agreement (SHA)** and **Share Purchase Agreement (SPA)**. Once signed and the wire transfer is received, the cap table is updated. The entrepreneur's finance creation process is complete, but the relationship with the investor is just beginning.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse "Angel Investor" and "Venture Capitalist" — the **distinction is the source of capital** (personal vs pooled institutional) and the **stage** (seed vs growth).
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **equity** with **grants** — government grants (e.g., SISFS) do not dilute ownership, but they often come with conditions and reporting requirements.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Writing "MUDRA is a bank" — MUDRA is a **refinancing institution** that lends through banks/NBFCs/MFIs; it is not itself a lender to the public.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Ignoring **valuation** in numerical problems — always compute post-money valuation before computing the equity percentage.
> 5. **Mark Loss Alert:** For 14-mark questions, students who write only a list of points without **comparative analysis** (investor vs financier) typically lose 2–3 marks for lack of depth.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Finance Creation (Investors/Financiers)**

- [ ] **Finance Creation** = the strategic process of raising external capital from investors (equity) and financiers (debt).
- [ ] **Investor** = owner who shares risk and reward. **Financier** = creditor with fixed contractual claims.
- [ ] **Angel Investor** = HNI investing personal funds at seed stage (₹25L–5Cr), accepts high risk, provides mentorship.
- [ ] **Venture Capitalist** = professional fund manager investing pooled institutional money at Series A+ stages (₹5–500Cr), demands board seats, exits via IPO/acquisition in 5–7 years, expects 10x returns on winners.
- [ ] **Private Equity** = late-stage investment, often control-oriented, used for mature profitable companies.
- [ ] **Crowdfunding** = many small investors fund via platforms; can be equity-based, debt-based, or donation-based.
